chunks.title: The Price of Integration
chunks.text: The Price of Integration South Africa's post-1994 opening reconnected the economy, but did not broaden opportunity: growth rose while jobs and income stayed concentrated..

chunks.title: The Question
chunks.text: The Question: Did opening up the country open it up for everyone? Short answer: not enough, and the rest of this essay shows why. After 1994, did South Africa integrate into the global economy faster than it created broad domestic opportunity, driving aggregate growth that ultimately left the majority behind? The RDP (1994) promised redistribution and a more active state; by 1996 policy had shifted to GEAR : tighter budgets, lower trade barriers, and inflation targets. The first Johannesburg Stock Exchange building, 1893. South Africa was tied to global finance long before 1994.

chunks.title: From the Ground
chunks.text: Before the charts, the places. The same national averages hide a gap you can see from the street. Inner city Johannesburg CBD: where the growth story is most visible. Source The Constitutional Court in Johannesburg: the institutions the new economy was built on. Source Outer city Gugulethu: the township behind the unemployment rate. Source Cato Crest: the settlement edge the growth never reached.

chunks.title: Timeline
chunks.text: Ten moments that opened the economy. Tracing the path: sanctions end, RDP becomes GEAR, global trade locks in, and commodities surge. The cards below map these turning points. Davos, 2005: being seen as a “normal” economy mattered, even as actual foreign investment stayed modest (see §4).

chunks.title: National Economy
chunks.text: Trade rose faster than what people earned. From docks to national lines You can see openness in trade flows before you feel it in paychecks: trade as a share of the economy explodes while income per person lags behind, all while unemployment remains painfully high. How to read Income per person and trade/GDP are set so 1990 = 100 (with macro lines back to 1960); the vertical axis uses a log scale so large swings in trade do not hide slower moves in income. Caution In these national data, higher trade moves alongside higher income per person, higher unemployment, and a higher top-income share. That pattern is real, but it does not prove trade caused any of them. Technical note A formal “long-run link” test between log income per person and trade/GDP does not find a stable long-run pairing here ( p ≈ 0.48 for Engle–Granger). Source World Development Indicators; trade-policy context in Sources. Indexed national indicators, 1990 = 100 (log scale) Income per person and trade/GDP from 1960, all indexed to 100 in 1990. Unemployment (ILO-style measure) High, persistent unemployment is the reason higher average income does not translate into jobs for everyone.

chunks.title: Sectors
chunks.text: The casualty was tradable work. Manufacturing and other tradable sectors shrank as openness rose; services did not absorb most displaced workers. South African manufacturing lost roughly a third of its GDP share between 1990 and 2024. Tradable sectors shed nearly twelve percentage points of employment share between 1999 and 2025. How to read The big line chart shows the share of employed adults working in tradable sectors ( ) vs the rest. The smaller chart traces manufacturing's value-added share of GDP from the WDI. Method Employment shares are weighted from Stats SA worker microdata: OHS 1999, LFS 2000–2007, QLFS 2008–2025. Year values average all available waves. Value-added shares come from the World Bank's WDI series (NV.IND.MANF.ZS). Caution Co-movement is not causation.

chunks.title: Sectors
chunks.text: The cleanest causal evidence remains Erten–Leight–Tregenna's district-level analysis; this is the macro sector pattern it implies. Tradable vs non-tradable employment share, 1999–2025 Each year is a weighted average across Stats SA labor-survey waves. Tradable =. Manufacturing's slice of GDP and employment Manufacturing VA share of GDP from 1960 (WDI); employment share from 1999 (LFS/QLFS). Why the sector cut is the sharper lens Erten, Leight and Tregenna (2019) compare districts that were more exposed to tariff cuts with districts that were less exposed. The more-exposed places saw weaker employment and formal-sector outcomes, with the heaviest costs falling on Black workers and women. The aggregate sector pattern here mirrors that story. Manufacturing, the most trade-exposed sector, lost ground in both output and jobs as openness rose. Services did not absorb the displaced workforce, which helps explain why national unemployment never fell back to the pre-1994 range. Read together, this section answers a sharper version of the essay's core question.

chunks.title: Sectors
chunks.text: Integration succeeded for capital flows and headline trade volumes, but it hollowed out the sectors that historically employed the broadest base of workers.

chunks.title: Inequality
chunks.text: The gains that remained went to the top tenth. The income that remained went mostly to the top. Who holds income and wealth The pattern is not subtle: by 2020 the best-off tenth of people held about 65% of pre-tax national income, while the bottom half received under 6%. Income WID shows the top 10%’s share already near 47% through the 1980s, then rising to about 65% by 2020. Wealth Wealth is more concentrated than yearly income: the top 10% held roughly 85–86% of household wealth from the 1980s onward. Source WID and Chatterjee–Czajka–Gethin. Pre-tax income shares (WID) Top-10% income share from 1980 (WID): ~47% through the 1980s → 65% in 2020. Wealth shares (WID) Wealth shares from 1980 (WID). See Chatterjee-Czajka-Gethin (2022). Voice from the record "Wealth concentration remained extraordinarily high despite the democratic transition and post-apartheid policy reform." Chatterjee, Czajka, and Gethin (2022) The core tension shows up in the inequality data. After 1994, openness did not come with a clearly fairer split of income. From 1994 on, the top tenth’s income share rose by roughly 18 percentage points. The bottom half’s share fell by about a third.

chunks.title: Inequality
chunks.text: Wealth is sharper still: the top 10% hold roughly 85% of household wealth. For cause and effect, the essay leans on Erten–Leight–Tregenna (2019). Districts more exposed to tariff cuts saw weaker jobs and wages, especially for Black workers and women.

chunks.title: Two Lives
chunks.text: Two lives, one transition. Pieter and Sipho are composite characters, not real people or survey respondents, built from apartheid spatial law, manufacturing decline, WID inequality shares, and district-level tariff exposure ( Erten–Leight–Tregenna ), then turned into five decision beats (1994 → 2020). Each choice nudges hidden scores for both characters; endings fall into bands from “falling behind” to “pulling ahead.” With four options per beat, there are 1,024 paths, yet Pieter lands secure ~88% of the time and Sipho only ~22%, because they start at different scores (capital vs. a trade-exposed wage), not because the menu differs. After each choice, a ~X% of South Africans could tag appears, estimated feasibility (QLFS, FinScope, stokvel, grant, migration proxies), not real survey choices. This interactive needs JavaScript. The short version: starting from 1994, the same economic choices tended to lift Pieter (white, Johannesburg, with access to capital) into the top income decile, while pushing Sipho (Black, from Gugulethu, in a trade-exposed factory job) toward retrenchment, displacement, and the informal economy.

chunks.title: Evidence
chunks.text: What survives a strict statistical test. A plain-English summary first; the full regression battery (hundreds of tests with diagnostics) is tucked away below for readers who want every estimate. Trade openness moves with top-income concentration. No clean structural break at the 1996 GEAR pivot. Trade does not robustly explain unemployment levels. Summary The project ran hundreds of regression specifications on the macro panel. After strict Benjamini–Hochberg corrections, two conclusions still hold: The move toward an open economy lines up with the decline of trade-exposed sectors. Manufacturing employment share and tradable employment share both fall robustly as trade openness rises (HAC t ≈ −2.5 and −3.8 over 26 years). Trade openness also tracks the rise of the top 1%'s income share. That is the one inequality-and-trade link the multiple-testing adjustments cannot dismiss. On the other side of the ledger, the Chow test does not find a clean structural break at the 1996 GEAR pivot, and trade does not robustly explain unemployment levels on its own. None of these single-country regressions are causal proof.

chunks.title: Evidence
chunks.text: For that, the essay leans on Erten–Leight–Tregenna's district-level evidence. Even so, they show which patterns are sturdy enough to take seriously. chunks.title: Map
chunks.text: One number hides nine very different provinces. Provincial narrow unemployment from 1997 - 2025 (harmonised ). Original finding National unemployment – → – Harmonised survey aggregate Provincial spread (max − min) – → – Gap between highest- and lowest-unemployment provinces Lowest vs highest province Western Cape – Eastern Cape – Both anchors rose; the gap between them widened. Unemployment rose, but so too did the spread of that unemployment. Under the surface, the spatial pattern of unemployment became more uneven across provinces, as shown in the graph below. Provincial unemployment spread over time Each point is the gap between the highest- and lowest-unemployment province in that quarter (percentage points). chunks.title: Map
chunks.text: First quarter vs latest quarter Lighter color = lower unemployment, darker color = higher unemployment Start Latest Method I calculated provincial rates from Stats SA microdata (narrow unemployment, survey weights), harmonising LFS and OHS for early years and QLFS thereafter. Official tables can differ by revision; treat long-run movement across survey redesigns as indicative. Sources lists P0211 and related entries under Datasets.

chunks.title: Conclusions
chunks.text: Integration succeeded. After 1994, South Africa successfully rejoined the world economy. The unresolved question is distribution: why those gains did not become broad employment and shared prosperity. Aggregate gains → uneven access Trade rose, sanctions-era isolation ended, and average income per person finished higher than in the early 1990s. On macro terms, integration delivered measurable gains. Macro progress → labor-market bottlenecks Those gains did not diffuse widely through the labor market. Unemployment remained structurally high, and income and wealth stayed heavily concentrated at the top. Correlation patterns → causal evidence National regressions here are correlational. The sharper causal signal comes from district-level evidence Erten–Leight–Tregenna : areas more exposed to tariff cuts saw weaker employment outcomes, with the largest burdens on Black workers and women. Past reintegration → next-phase inclusion The lesson is not to retreat from global integration. It is to pair openness with employment-centered industrial policy, labor-market activation, and place-based investment where exclusion is persistent.

chunks.title: Conclusions
chunks.text: South Africa’s post-1994 transition solved reintegration faster than inclusion. The country became more open and somewhat richer on average, but not broadly secure in jobs and income. The next phase is not less integration. It is better-governed integration so gains in trade and investment translate into mass opportunity, not just aggregate improvement. What this project adds A new assembly of existing data. Most of these numbers already existed, but they live in different databases. This project merges World Bank macro series (WDI), inequality shares (WID), and harmonised labor surveys (OHS, LFS, QLFS) into one timeline keyed by year, then adds tradable sector shares, a provincial unemployment map that updates with each survey wave, and charts that reach back before 1990. No single source publishes that combination as one view. The site and the regressions behind it use that merged panel so openness, sector loss, inequality, and geography can be read in the same place.

chunks.title: Conclusions
chunks.text: On my map, national unemployment rose about 10 percentage points from 1997 Q4 to 2025 Q4, while the gap between the lowest- and highest-unemployment provinces widened by roughly 8 points, integration strained inclusion geographically, not just on average.

chunks.title: Sources
chunks.text: How to check this essay. Every claim above is either a direct calculation on the linked dataset or a citation to a source listed below. Datasets World Development Indicators (Databank) : exports, GDP, population, etc. World Inequality Database, South Africa : income and wealth shares. WIID (UNU-WIDER) : harmonised Gini / Palma. Stats SA, Statistical release P0211 (QLFS) : official publications and methodology. Statistics South Africa : producer of QLFS/LFS/OHS microdata used in analysis scripts. Provincial map (§9): Narrow unemployment by province/quarter from harmonised Stats SA LFS / OHS / QLFS microdata (my aggregates; not official published tables). Files & coverage Stats SA and DataFirst (UCT) survey microdata. Some pre-2008 waves omitted where labor fields are not comparable. OHS (1994–1999): Microdata via DataFirst (UCT). Follow DataFirst/CC terms; cite Statistics South Africa as producer. Policy history RDP white paper (1994) : Government Gazette No. GEAR (1996) : National Treasury consolidated chapters (PDF). Adelzadeh (1996), Transformation 31 : archived record for the RDP → GEAR commentary. WTO trade policy review, SACU (2023) : tariff and trade regime.

chunks.title: Sources
chunks.text: Mayekiso & Lamla (2025) : liberalisation, privatisation, labor. ILRIG, political economy of the transition : popular-education course hub. World Bank (2021), South Africa Economic Update (jobs & inequality / post-COVID). Causal evidence cited Erten, Leight & Tregenna (2019), Journal of International Economics : local labor markets after tariff cuts. Driver (2019), UNU-WIDER working paper : manufacturing and trade liberalisation. Uhuru (2020), UCT (OpenUCT) : synthetic control and sanctions. Evenett (2002), Scottish Journal of Political Economy : sanctions and exports. Chatterjee, Czajka & Gethin (2022), World Bank Economic Review : wealth inequality. Scope of claims (short version) Fair to say: After 1994, South Africa opened more while unemployment stayed high and income and wealth remained concentrated. That is a descriptive read of the national charts and cited surveys. Fair to say: On this site’s macro panel, two associations survive Benjamini–Hochberg correction: trade openness with declining tradable-sector employment shares and with rising top-1% income share (see §8). Fair to say: Provincial unemployment is spatially uneven.

chunks.title: Sources
chunks.text: My map (§9) uses aggregates I built from harmonised Stats SA microdata; treat long runs across survey redesigns as indicative. Fair to say: On my map, the provincial spread (gap between lowest- and highest-unemployment provinces) widened by roughly 8 percentage points from 1997 Q4 to 2025 Q4 while national unemployment also rose, descriptive spatial divergence that complements district-level studies cited below, not proof of causation on its own. Not fair to claim: That national regressions or these charts alone prove trade caused inequality, sector decline, or unemployment. Stronger causal claims rely on outside studies cited in Sources (e.g. Not fair to claim: That GEAR (1996) marks a clean structural break in these national series (the Chow test here does not support that), or that trade openness by itself explains unemployment levels in the macro panel. Not fair to claim: Perfect comparability across inequality databases (different methods and end years) or across every map quarter (some waves lack comparable labor fields). Not fair to claim: That Two Lives (Pieter and Sipho) are real individuals or direct microdata extracts.

chunks.title: Sources
chunks.text: They are composite illustrations built from the essay’s evidence and history. Limits of the evidence (most important first) Few years per series. At most about 35 annual points. Statistical precision is limited even when p-values look tiny. Many rows fail the Durbin–Watson check (below 1.5), meaning errors cluster over time. Adjusted standard errors help, but filtering those rows is wise when you want cleaner reads. This page estimates hundreds of equations. Bonferroni and BH adjustments punish that; surviving BH is the credibility bar used here. Correlation is not policy proof. For stronger causal claims, this essay leans on outside work: sanctions synthetic controls, district labor markets after tariff cuts, and wealth distribution accounting (Chatterjee et al., 2022). WIID-based Gini fades after 2017; WDI Gini is thin. Treat cross-series comparisons as suggestive, not exact. How to cite this page APA: Hallur, S. The Price of Integration: South Africa after 1994.

chunks.title: Building Back Better Covid Jobs
chunks.text: ## Bibliographic details - **Publisher:** World Bank, Washington DC - **Series:** South Africa Economic Update, Edition 13 - **Year:** 2021 - **Length:** ~55 pages (Executive Summary + Part 1 Recent Economic Developments + Part 2 Labour Markets & COVID impacts) ## Purpose The 13th edition in the World Bank's semi-regular SA Economic Update series. Provides the Bank's diagnostic of the COVID-19 shock on the SA economy and argues the pandemic is best understood as an *amplifier* of pre-existing structural weaknesses rather than a standalone shock. ## Key stylised facts it documents - **GDP**: Real GDP contracted **7% in 2020**, the deepest recession since the end of apartheid, exceeded among major emerging economies only by Argentina, India, Mexico. - **Fiscal**: Fiscal deficit widened to **12.9% of GDP** in 2020/21; public debt rose to **78.8% of GDP**. Debt-service burden approaching **5% of GDP**. - **Labour market**: ~**1.5 million jobs** lost by end-2020; wages for the still-employed fell 10–15%; average working-age South African was ~18% worse off at end-2020 vs start. - **Unemployment**: rose by >1 million to ~7 million unemployed; **unemployment rate 33%**, a record high. - **Inactivity**: ~17 million working-age South Africans not in the labour force; ~3 million classified as discouraged. - **Self-employment**: only **~10% of SA jobs**, versus ~30% in peer upper-middle-income economies, identified as a key under-exploited margin. ## Three interconnected challenges (the Bank's framing) 1. **Growth**, the 2010s was a "lost decade"; per-capita GDP declined in real terms from 2015; broad-based structural reform (energy, freight rail, ports, regulation) needed to restart private investment. 2. **Jobs**, persistent high unemployment, low participation, very low self-employment; the pandemic hit poorer workers harder in employment losses, hit richer workers harder on wages. 3. **Debt**, entered the crisis already fiscally stressed; rising contingent liabilities from SOEs, especially **Eskom**; SA's deep domestic financial sector provides resilience but fiscal path is unsustainable absent reform. ## Policy proposals (Part 2) Four categories of intervention to strengthen job recovery: 1. **Strengthen labour-market linkages of temporary government programmes** - Expand and deepen the **Employment Tax Incentive (ETI)** targeting youth and women. - Extend the **Temporary Employer/Employee Relief Scheme (TERS)** for lockdown-affected sectors until recovery consolidates. 2. **Consider a time-bound moratorium** (12–18 months) on regulations that raise the real cost of labour, explicitly linked to the pandemic and vulnerable worker protection. 3. **Revisit entrepreneurship and self-employment policy** - Relax legal constraints on informal / micro enterprises. - Scale up combined entrepreneurial-training + start-up-grant programmes. - Headline claim: raising SA's self-employment rate to peer averages could **halve** the unemployment rate. 4. **Improve governance of active labour-market programmes** - Incorporate job-search and training into EPWP and the Presidential Employment Stimulus. - Incentivise labour intermediation services. - Use UIF funds to supplement part-time wages. - Build monitoring and evaluation for programme targeting. ## Distributional findings - **Employment losses** concentrated in the **bottom 40%** of the income distribution, low-skill, informal-adjacent jobs in trade, accommodation, and transport were hardest hit by lockdowns. - **Wage losses** concentrated at the **top of the distribution**, richer workers kept their jobs but took larger pay cuts. - Net effect: inequality rose, because the employment channel dominates the wage channel in income terms. ## Methodology and data - Relies on **Stats SA QLFS** micro-data for labour-market analysis (see [[quarterly-labour-force-survey]]). - Uses **NIDS-CRAM** (National Income Dynamics Study Coronavirus Rapid Mobile Survey) for wage-level detail during lockdowns. - World

chunks.title: Dataset Sa Wdi Panel
chunks.text: ## File `raw/data/sa_wdi_panel.csv`, 34 rows × 7 columns, 1990 → 2023, one row per calendar year. ## Columns | column | meaning | WDI code (source) | |---|---|---| | `year` | Calendar year |, | | `gini` | Gini index (survey years only) | `SI.POV.GINI` | | `gdp_pc_constant_usd` | GDP per capita, constant 2015 USD | `NY.GDP.PCAP.KD` | | `gdp_growth_pct` | Real GDP growth, % | `NY.GDP.MKTP.KD.ZG` | | `trade_pct_gdp` | Trade (exports + imports) as % of GDP | `NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS` | | `fdi_net_inflows_pct_gdp` | FDI net inflows, BoP, % of GDP | `BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS` | | `unemployment_ilo_pct` | Unemployment, ILO-modelled, % of labour force (15+) | `SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS` | ## Key stylised facts visible in the panel - **Trade openness** roughly doubled over 1990–2008: 38% → 66% of GDP, then fluctuated 50–65% post-GFC. - **FDI is volatile**, mostly 0–3% of GDP, with a single spike to 9.7% in 2021 (Prosus/Naspers unbundling). - **Unemployment** rose from ~22% in the mid-1990s to ~34% in 2021, with a pandemic-driven jump in 2020–21. - **GDP per capita** grew from about USD 4 500 (1990) to a peak of USD 6 170 (2013), then stagnated. - **Gini** is only available in survey years (1993, 2000, 2005, 2008, 2010, 2014, 2022); values hover around 0.60–0.65 and are consistent with the top-decile income shares reported in the WID data and in [[wealth-inequality-lab-south-africa|Chatterjee-Czajka-Gethin (2021)]]. ## Usage in the project - Drives the **national time-series chart** on the website (gdp_pc, unemployment, trade, FDI). - Provides the national-level benchmark against which provincial Stats SA QLFS numbers are compared in the interactive map. - Serves as the reconciliation set for cross-checks against WID and Stats SA series. ## Caveats - Unemployment is the **ILO-modelled** (WDI) series, which applies ILO harmonisation to national QLFS inputs; it therefore differs **in definition** from the **strict (official) unemployment rate** published by Stats SA in the [[stats-sa-qlfs-p0211-2023q3|QLFS Q3 2023]] and [[stats-sa-qlfs-p0211-2025q1|QLFS Q1 2025]] releases. Do not compare the two definitions without explicit alignment. - Gini has gaps; do not interpolate without documenting the imputation. - Trade openness is nominal, real openness trends (accounting for terms-of-trade effects) require deflated series from SARB Quarterly Bulletin. ## Sources - World Bank, *World Development Indicators* (WDI), via api.worldbank.org. - Snapshot taken April 10, 2026, see the file's mtime for the exact timestamp.

chunks.title: Dataset Wgi Underlying Sources
chunks.text: ## File `raw/data/raw_underlying_sources_1996_2024.xlsx`, six worksheets corresponding to the six Worldwide Governance Indicators (WGI) dimensions. ~24 MB. The variant `raw/data/Raw Data from Underlying Data Sources (1996-2024).xlsx` appears to be a duplicate; only one needs to be kept in the raw corpus. ## Sheets (one per WGI dimension) | Sheet | Dimension | Meaning | |---|---|---| | `VA` | Voice and Accountability | Perceptions of citizens' ability to participate in government | | `PV` | Political Stability & Absence of Violence | Likelihood of political instability / politically motivated violence | | `GE` | Government Effectiveness | Quality of public services, civil service, policy formulation | | `RQ` | Regulatory Quality | Ability of government to formulate and implement sound regulation | | `RL` | Rule of Law | Confidence in rules of society; enforcement of contracts, property | | `CC` | Control of Corruption | Extent to which public power is exercised for private gain | ## Columns (shared schema across all six sheets) | Column | Meaning | |---|---| | `DataSourceCode` | Short code for the underlying survey / index (e.g. AFR = Afrobarometer; ADB = African Development Bank CPIA) | | `Organization` | Publisher of the underlying source | | `DataSource` | Name of the underlying instrument | | `indicator` | Specific question / sub-indicator from the underlying source | | `econ_code` | ISO-3 country code | | `econ_name` | Country name | | `region` | WB region | | `income_class` | WB income class | | `production_year` | Year the observation pertains to | | `wgi_dimension` | Dimension code (`va`, `pv`, `ge`, `rq`, `rl`, `cc`) | | `value` | The underlying source's raw value | | `minimum`, `maximum` | Raw scale bounds for `value` | | `orientation` | +1 = higher is better; −1 = higher is worse (used for sign alignment in WGI aggregation) | ## What makes this file valuable The public WGI **aggregate** series (e.g. "Control of Corruption, percentile rank") hides the underlying disagreement among sources. This file contains the **full raw inputs**, all 30+ source × indicator × country × year combinations that Kaufmann & Kraay aggregate into each WGI dimension. Analysts can: - Drill from the aggregate back to the source-level observation (e.g. which Afrobarometer question changed SA's VA score between 2015 and 2020). - Reconstruct aggregate scores using alternative weightings or subsets (e.g. drop Afrobarometer; keep only business surveys). - Quantify measurement uncertainty by computing the dispersion across sources for a given country-year. - Run cross-country governance analyses matched to inequality / trade / growth data in the same panel. ## Usage in the project - Provides the **institutional-quality** covariates for any synthetic-control or gravity-equation extension (à la [[sanctions-synthetic-control-south-africa|Uhuru 2020]] or [[sanctions-impact-south-african-exports|Evenett 2002]]). - Anchors the narrative that SA's governance scores **eroded** over the 2010s, especially CC (Control of Corruption) and GE (Government Effectiveness), relevant to the [[political-economy-of-transition|political-economy]] account of state capture. - Source of record for the governance dimension of the underlying Python panel used in the website. ## Caveats - Raw source scales are heterogeneous; comparisons across `DataSourceCode` require rescaling using `minimum`, `maximum`, and `orientation`. - Some country-year cells are missing for some sources; WGI's aggregation uses an unobserved-components model to handle this. - The file is labelled 1996–2024 but individual sources have narrower coverage. - The identical file with a different name suggests an upload duplicate; consolidate before distribution. ## Sources - World Bank, *Worldwide Governance Indicators* (Kaufmann, Kraay & Mastruzzi), 2024 update, https://www.worldbank.org/en/publication/worldwide-governance-indicators.

chunks.title: Dataset Wid South Africa
chunks.text: ## Files - `raw/data/WID_data_ZA.csv`, 1 063 336 observations × 7 columns (~41 MB). The full WID data dump for South Africa. - `raw/data/WID_metadata_ZA.csv`, 1 660 variable × age × population rows × 19 columns. Variable-level metadata with sources, methods, and extrapolation notes. - `raw/data/WID_countries.csv`, WID country catalogue (alpha-2, titlename, shortname, region). Useful only for joining to other WID country files. ## Data model `WID_data_ZA.csv`: | column | meaning | |---|---| | `country` | ISO-2 (`ZA`) | | `variable` | WID variable code (e.g. `sptinc992j` = share of pre-tax national income, adults, year) | | `percentile` | e.g. `p0p100`, `p90p100`, `p99p100`, `p0p50` | | `year` | Calendar year (data go back to 1820 for a few series; most start 1950s–1990s) | | `value` | The data point (monetary values in ZAR, shares in 0–1) | | `age` | `992` = adults, `999` = all ages | | `pop` | `i` = individuals, `j` = equal-split adults, `f` = females | `WID_metadata_ZA.csv`, for every `variable × age × pop`, provides: - `simpledes` / `technicaldes`, human-readable description. - `longtype`, average / share / threshold / total / number / ratio. - `unit`, e.g. ZAR. - `source` and `method`, free text describing the fiscal + survey + national-accounts inputs. - `extrapolation`, year-by-year imputation method, often linking to SA-SARS, OECD SNA, UN SNA, and the DINA guidelines. ## What's in it (subject coverage) - **Distributional National Accounts (DINA)**: pre-tax and post-tax national income shares and averages by percentile (e.g. top 10%, top 1%, top 0.1% shares). - **Personal wealth** shares and averages (`hweal` family). - **National-accounts subcomponents**: consumption of fixed capital, mixed income, compensation of employees, operating surplus, by institutional sector (corporations, government, households) and age cut-off. - **Demographic and price variables**: population, mortality-adjusted adults, PPP, CPI, GDP deflator. - **Factor-share estimates** derived from the Dietrich-Nievas-Odersky-Piketty-Somanchi (2025) Technical Note on factor shares. ## Canonical series to extract for the project For the project's inequality narrative, the following percentile × variable pairs are the most useful: - `sptinc992j` × `p99p100`, top 1% share of pre-tax national income, adults, equal-split. - `sptinc992j` × `p90p100`, top 10% share. - `sptinc992j` × `p0p50`, bottom 50% share. - `shweal992j` × `p99p100`, `p90p100`, `p0p50`, corresponding wealth shares. - `aptinc992j` × `pall`, average pre-tax income, adults (for levels). - `ahweal992j` × `pall`, average wealth. These underlie the [[wealth-inequality-lab-south-africa|WIL (2021)]] figures and are the **source** for the most-cited South-African inequality statistics (e.g. "top 10% hold ~85% of wealth"). ## Caveats - Pre-1980 estimates are back-extrapolated from fiscal data and should be treated as indicative, not measurement. - 2019–2024 figures are frequently imputed from trend components (see the `extrapolation` column for every variable × year); for policy claims about the most recent years, prefer Stats SA / SARS microdata where available. - Distinguish **national accounts** vs **household survey** vs **tax** inputs; for SA, the post-2010 WID rests heavily on SARS PIT microdata (via SA-TIED). - Currency is **ZAR**, mostly in current-year values; use the WID `inyixx999i` deflator before comparing years. ## Sources - World Inequality Database (WID.world), accessed April 1, 2026. - DINA methodology: *Distributional National Accounts Guidelines 2020*, WID. - Factor-share series: Dietrich, Nievas, Odersky, Piketty & Somanchi (2025), *Extending WID National Accounts Series*.

chunks.title: Dataset Wiid 2025
chunks.text: ## File `raw/data/WIID-29APR2025.xlsx`, one sheet (`Sheet1`), 80 columns. A cross-country panel of income-distribution statistics maintained by UNU-WIDER. The 29 April 2025 release is the most recent as of the project's cut-off. ## Key columns (by group) **Identification / geography:** - `id`, `country`, `c3` (ISO-3), `c2` (ISO-2), `year` - `region_un`, `region_un_sub`, `region_wb`, `eu`, `oecd`, `incomegroup` **Headline distribution metrics:** - `gini`, Gini coefficient (0–100 scale in WIID). - `palma`, Palma ratio (top 10% / bottom 40%). - `ratio_top20bottom20`, 80/20 ratio. - `ge0`, `ge1`, `ge2`, generalised entropy indices (MLD, Theil T, Theil T squared). - `a025`, `a050`, `a075`, `a1`, `a2`, Atkinson indices at aversion parameters ε = 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.0, 2.0. **Quantile shares:** - `q1 … q5`, quintile shares. - `d1 … d10`, decile shares. - `bottom40`, `bottom5`, `top5`, auxiliary. **Survey metadata (very rich):** - `resource`, `resource_detailed`, income concept (market, disposable, consumption, earnings). - `scale`, `scale_detailed`, equivalised, per capita, household total. - `sharing_unit`, `reference_unit`, household vs individual. - `areacovr`, `popcovr`, geographic and population coverage. - `source`, `source_detailed`, `source_comments`, `survey`, `link`. - `quality`, `quality_score`, `revision`, WIDER's quality grading. **Macro anchors:** - `mean`, `median`, in local currency; plus `mean_usd`, `median_usd`. - `currency`, `reference_period`, `exchangerate`. - `gdp`, `population`. **Decomposition (for multi-observation countries):** - `pop_share`, `inc_share`. - `w_ge0` … `w_a1`, `b_ge0` … `b_a1`, within/between group GE and Atkinson. - `wiidcompanion`, flag for WIID Companion inequality series. ## South African coverage WIID contains multiple income-distribution observations per year for SA, with variation by source (IES, LCS, NIDS) and concept (market, disposable, consumption, per capita vs equivalised). This is the right data source to **reconcile different SA Gini estimates** (e.g. 0.63 vs 0.67 vs 0.68) because the database carries the conceptual metadata needed to explain the gap. ## Usage - Cross-country benchmarking of SA inequality against comparators. - Methodological reconciliation of Gini estimates across surveys / concepts. - Cross-validation against WID percentile shares and World Bank Poverty and Inequality Platform (PIP). ## Caveats - WIID is **a harmonised catalogue, not a harmonised series**. Each observation preserves its original source concept; users must filter on `resource`, `scale`, and `sharing_unit` before comparing across countries or years. - The `quality_score` (1–5) should be used as a filter; SA observations vary in quality. - Population-weighted regional averages that ignore these filters produce misleading headlines. ## Sources - UNU-WIDER, *World Income Inequality Database (WIID)*, version 29 April 2025, https://www.wider.unu.edu/database/wiid.

chunks.title: Gear 1996 Macroeconomic Strategy
chunks.text: ## Bibliographic details - **Publisher:** Department of Finance, Republic of South Africa, June 1996 - **Ministers:** Trevor Manuel (Finance), Gill Marcus (Deputy), Maria Ramos (Acting DG) - **Technical team:** Andre Roux, Iraj Abedian, Alan Hirsch, Stephen Gelb, Brian Kahn, Servaas van der Berg, Luiz Pereira da Silva & Richard Ketley (World Bank), etc. - **Type:** Primary government strategy document, ~25 pages ## Headline targets (the "integrated scenario", 1996–2000) - **GDP growth:** reach 6.1% by 2000 (avg. 4.2%). - **New jobs:** 400 000 per year by 2000 (avg. 270 000). - **Fiscal deficit:** reduce from 5.1% to 3.0% of GDP. - **Average tariff:** from 10% to 6% of imports. - **Inflation:** held below 10%. - **Real non-gold export growth:** ~8.4% avg. - **Real private investment growth:** ~11.7% avg. ## Core logic > "Sustained growth on a higher plane requires a transformation towards a competitive outward-oriented economy." The strategy is an orthodox stabilisation-plus-liberalisation package: 1. **Fiscal tightening**, faster deficit reduction to crowd in private investment and ease pressure on interest rates. 2. **Monetary policy** focused on inflation reduction, with **positive real interest rates**. 3. **Exchange-rate policy**, keep the real effective rate stable at a competitive level after the 1996 depreciation. 4. **Further relaxation of exchange controls** (gradual dismantling). 5. **Accelerated tariff reductions** beyond WTO commitments, to compensate for the depreciation and contain input prices. 6. **Tax incentives** for new, labour-absorbing investment (accelerated depreciation; tax holidays). 7. **Public-sector asset restructuring**, partial privatisation / equity partners in Telkom, transport, energy. 8. **Expansionary public infrastructure** programme to "crowd in" private investment. 9. **"Regulated flexibility"** in the labour market, differentiated minimum wages, lower rates for young trainees, greater bargaining flexibility. 10. **National social agreement** to keep wage and price increases within productivity growth. ## How it differs from the [[reconstruction-and-development-programme|RDP]] - GEAR treats **redistribution as an outcome of growth**, not a co-equal objective. - **Employment** is driven mainly by private investment responding to lower interest rates and tariff reductions, a crowding-out / crowding-in story. - **Trade** policy is to "consolidate and accelerate" liberalisation, not to use tariffs for industrial policy. - **Labour-market** posture is "flexibility" rather than protection. ## Model and projections - Uses (primarily) the South African Reserve Bank macro model plus a Department of Finance expenditure model. - Projects that if wage moderation, fiscal consolidation, and additional FDI materialise, the "integrated scenario" delivers 4.2% avg growth, 2.4% average current-account deficit, and 270 000 jobs/year. - The "base scenario" (with slower reform) is projected at ~2.8% growth and ~104 000 jobs/year. ## Political economy - Adopted without extensive consultation with labour (COSATU, SACP) or civil society; widely read as a unilateral pivot to neoliberal macro policy, hence the [[rdp-to-gear-transition|Adelzadeh (1996) critique]] and the broader [[political-economy-of-transition|political-economy of transition]] literature. - Many of its targets (6% growth, 400k jobs/year) were **not met**. Tariff and fiscal targets largely were. ## Significance for the project GEAR is the operational pivot of post-apartheid macroeconomic policy and the empirical anchor of most subsequent liberalisation studies (Erten-Leight-Tregenna, Driver, Edwards-Lawrence, Rodrik). It defines the period in which tariffs fall, exchange controls loosen, deficits tighten, and the manufacturing base begins its contraction, the stylised facts that the rest of the knowledge base tries to explain. ## Sources - `raw/readings/gear_1996_macroeconomic_strategy.pdf`, Republic of South Africa, Department of Finance, *Growth,

chunks.title: Mayekiso Trade Liberalisation Privatisation
chunks.text: ## Bibliographic details - **Authors:** Cwenga Mayekiso, Musawenkosi Lamla (Walter Sisulu University) - **Published:** *Insights into Regional Development* 7(4), December 2025, 262–277 - **DOI:** 10.70132/j4269338243 (open access) ## Research question Over 1994–2024, how did trade liberalisation and privatisation affect **employment**, **manufacturing output**, and **inequality** in South Africa? ## Method - **Mixed-methods retrospective:** - Trend analysis of public macro data (Stats SA, World Bank, DTIC, Engineering News). - Semi-structured interviews with 15 MA Political Science students. - Qualitative coding of perceptions of deindustrialisation, job insecurity, skill mismatch. ## Stylised facts the paper documents - **Manufacturing jobs:** 1.4 million (2005) → 1.09 million (2021), a loss of ~309 000. - **Manufacturing share of GDP:** 19.1% → 13.2%. - **Unemployment (Q1 2024):** 32.9% (official); youth (15–34) 45.5%. - **Gini:** roughly 0.63 → 0.67 over the period. - **Tariff regime:** sustained reductions after 1994 under WTO, SADC, and EU-SA TDCA commitments. ## Theoretical framing Triangulates three lenses: 1. **Premature deindustrialisation** (Rodrik 2015, 2016, 2021), developing countries losing manufacturing share at lower income levels than historical industrialisers. 2. **Political economy of labour and distribution**, trade effects mediated by labour-market institutions, bargaining, redistribution policies. 3. **State capability / new industrial policy**, prescriptive: DTIC reviews, EV and automotive white papers (2023–24), localisation. ## Core argument Trade liberalisation in SA, **unaccompanied by effective industrial and labour policies**, exacerbated structural vulnerability: labour-intensive manufacturing (textiles, apparel, metal products) contracted sharply; capital-intensive export-oriented sectors (chemicals, autos) stabilised but did not absorb displaced workers. The result is a labour-absorption paradox, openness without broad-based employment gains, and entrenched inequality. Privatisation, which the ILRIG handbook and other heterodox literature treat as a parallel lever (e.g. Telkom, Transnet, Eskom restructuring), is argued to have similarly shifted risks and rents rather than delivered efficient service expansion. ## Policy recommendations - Targeted **reindustrialisation** via localisation, procurement preferences, and sector-specific incentives. - **Skills and labour-market policies** aligned with labour-intensive sector demand. - **Redistributive policies** to offset the skill bias of openness. - A **mixed economy** balancing openness and strategic state intervention. ## Strengths and limitations - Valuable **synthesis of 30 years of stylised facts** through Stats SA / DTIC / World Bank sources, with clear documentation. - Qualitative component is limited (15 postgraduate students, convenience sample) and should be read as illustrative rather than representative. - Not causal: does not separate trade liberalisation from global automation, offshoring, or domestic policy failures (electricity, logistics, crime). ## How it connects Provides the **30-year retrospective** framing for the knowledge base, tying together: - [[trade-liberalization-local-labor-markets-south-africa|Erten-Leight-Tregenna (2018)]], causal local-labour-market evidence for 1994–2004. - [[trade-liberalization-sa-manufacturing|Driver (2019)]], firm-level evidence for mark-ups and exchange rates. - [[wealth-inequality-in-south-africa|Chatterjee-Czajka-Gethin (2021)]], inequality's wealth dimension. - [[political-economy-of-transition|ILRIG (2023) handbook]], political-economy narrative. ## Sources - `raw/readings/mayekiso_trade_liberalisation_privatisation_post_apartheid.pdf`, Mayekiso, C. and Lamla, M. (2025). "Evaluating the socioeconomic consequences of trade liberalisation and privatisation (1994–2024) in post-apartheid South Africa." *Insights into Regional Development* 7(4): 262–277.

chunks.title: Political Economy Of Transition Handbook Part1
chunks.text: ## Bibliographic details - **Publisher:** International Labour, Research & Information Group (ILRIG), January 2023 - **Author:** Dale T. McKinley - **Cover design:** Design for Development; printed by Hansa Print - **Type:** Political education handbook (~72 pages), Part 1 of a two-part "Handbook of Struggle" series ## Purpose An activist-oriented political-economy history of SA from the late 1970s through the first twenty-plus years of post-apartheid rule. Intended for popular education, for worker and community movements, but closely sourced and empirically grounded. ## Narrative arc 1. **Late 1970s / early 1980s:** Apartheid-capitalism in crisis. Gathering strikes, the rise of the UDF, COSATU's formation (1985), capital flight. The Minerals-Energy Complex (MEC) is the structural frame used throughout. 2. **Mid–late 1980s:** State of emergency (1985 onward), sanctions, divestment, collapse of the rand, apartheid's economic exhaustion. White capital begins to seek negotiated exit. 3. **1990–1992:** Unbanning of the ANC, release of Mandela, talks about talks. The policy agenda, in parallel with the negotiated transition, shifts from the "Harare-era" socialist rhetoric towards a more market-compatible programme. 4. **1992–1994:** Negotiated political settlement. "Sunset clauses" protect apartheid-era civil servants, judges, and property. The **economic settlement** is left largely intact. 5. **1994:** First democratic election; RDP adopted as the founding policy framework (see [[rdp-1994|RDP summary]]). 6. **Mid-1990s:** RDP Office closed (1996); **GEAR** adopted (1996) without negotiation with labour or civil society, the handbook treats this as the pivotal moment of the "neoliberal capture" of the ANC (see [[rdp-to-gear-transition|Adelzadeh 1996]] for the contemporary economists' critique and [[gear-1996-macroeconomic-strategy|GEAR summary]]). 7. **Late 1990s / late 2000s:** Privatisation, financialisation, rising inequality, Marikana, state capture. The handbook reads these as downstream effects of the 1994–96 economic settlement. ## The "house" metaphor SA is described as a house whose **political landlords** changed (NP → ANC) but whose **economic landlords** (white capital, increasingly financialised) were allowed to keep the penthouse. The handbook's key claim: "liberation turns out to be little more than a political and racially framed shifting of the capitalist veranda chairs without a corresponding transformation of socio-economic foundations." ## Key analytical concepts (glossary) - **Minerals-Energy Complex (MEC):** mining + heavy industry + energy + finance as a distinctive accumulation regime; used to argue that SA's economic path was shaped by the sectoral power of mining-finance-electricity long before and after 1994. - **Neo-liberalism:** the ideology that displaced the RDP's Keynesian-developmental frame; associated in this narrative with GEAR, exchange-control liberalisation, asset restructuring, and privatisation. - **Corporatisation / privatisation / commodification:** the operational levers through which state assets and services (Telkom, water, electricity, transport) were reshaped post-1994. - **Financialisation:** capital allocation shifting from productive investment to financial speculation and debt-driven consumption. ## Strengths and limits - Rich narrative of **class formation**, **labour mobilisation**, and **institutional politics** that macro-econometric studies rarely address. - Heterodox, anti-capitalist frame, strong ideological perspective, openly signalled; reads best in dialogue with mainstream empirical work. - Light on quantitative detail; best paired with [[wealth-inequality-in-south-africa|Chatterjee-Czajka-Gethin (2021)]] for the stock-inequality numbers and the [[rdp-to-gear-transition|Adelzadeh 1996]] critique for the macro-policy arithmetic. ## How it connects This is the knowledge base's **political-economy backbone**. Treat it as the narrative skeleton onto which the quantitative

chunks.title: Rdp 1994
chunks.text: ## Bibliographic details - **Author:** African National Congress (ANC), under Nelson Mandela - **Year:** 1994 (pre-election ANC policy framework; followed by the September 1994 RDP White Paper) - **Type:** Primary policy document, ~146 pages ## Core claim The RDP is framed as an "integrated, coherent socio-economic policy framework" to eradicate apartheid and build a democratic, non-racial, non-sexist South Africa. It breaks with the orthodox "growth first, redistribute later" view by arguing that growth and redistribution are **parts of a single process**, linked by an infrastructural programme (housing, water, electricity, telecoms, transport, health, education) that meets basic needs and simultaneously unlocks productive capacity. ## Six basic principles 1. Integrated and sustainable programme. 2. A **people-driven** process (development as participation, not delivery). 3. Peace and security for all (non-partisan security forces; legitimate judiciary). 4. **Nation-building**, rejects a "first-world / third-world" dualism and trickle-down. 5. Link reconstruction and development (growth + redistribution as one integrated programme, with infrastructure as the key link). 6. Democratisation of the state and society (beyond periodic elections). ## Five key programmes 1. **Meeting basic needs**, jobs, land, housing (1 million+ houses in five years), water and sanitation for all, electrification of 2.5 million homes, clean-water access for 12 million without it, universal primary healthcare, social security, land redistribution. 2. **Developing human resources**, education and training, arts and culture, sport, youth development. 3. **Building the economy**, industry, trade and commerce, resource-based industries, infrastructure upgrading, financial-sector reform, labour rights, Southern African regional policy. 4. **Democratising state and society**, constituent assembly, security-sector reform, restructuring the public sector and parastatals, strengthening civil society and local government. 5. **Implementing the RDP**, coordinating structures, planning frameworks, financing. ## Diagnostic framing - Apartheid produced one of the world's most unequal income distributions; underdevelopment of bantustans and townships contrasted with white-owned commercial farms and well-resourced suburbs. - Conglomerate concentration; cheap-labour policies; employment segregation; under-investment in R&D and training in protected industries. - The RDP explicitly rejects "piecemeal and uncoordinated policies" and trickle-down development. ## Economic posture - Keynesian-developmental in tone: a **leading and enabling** role for the state in a mixed economy. - Argues that meeting basic needs builds productive capacity and export potential. - The growth path "must ensure more equitable distribution of incomes" (quoted later as a benchmark against [[gear-strategy|GEAR]]). - Commits to a **living wage** as a precondition for the required level of growth. ## Significance The RDP is the founding macro-policy vision of the post-apartheid state. Within eighteen months it was partly displaced (critics say effectively replaced) by [[gear-strategy|GEAR]] (1996), which adopted a more neo-liberal fiscal-deficit-targeting, trade-liberalising stance. The distance between the 1994 RDP and the 1996 GEAR is the pivot of much of the subsequent [[political-economy-of-transition|political-economy literature]] (see e.g. [[rdp-to-gear-transition|Adelzadeh 1996]]). ## Sources - `raw/readings/rdp_1994.pdf`, ANC, *The Reconstruction and Development Programme: A Policy Framework*, 1994.

chunks.title: Rdp To Gear Transition
chunks.text: ## Bibliographic details - **Author:** Asghar Adelzadeh (National Institute for Economic Policy, commissioned by COSATU) - **Published:** *Transformation* 31 (1996), pp. 66–95 - **Type:** Peer-reviewed critique / commentary ## Thesis The 1996 [[gear-strategy|GEAR document]] represents a **wholesale abandonment** of the original Keynesian-developmental thrust of the [[reconstruction-and-development-programme|RDP]] in favour of an IMF-style neo-liberal stabilisation package. The paper argues GEAR is "analytically flawed, empirically unsupportable, historically unsuitable" and will fail to deliver the RDP's objectives on growth, employment, and redistribution. ## Key lines of critique 1. **Modelling problems.** GEAR's "four-model" claim is unsupported: reported results come from the SARB model only, which is opaque, was used during apartheid without restructuring, and cannot produce sectoral employment, income distribution, or the effects of government-expenditure restructuring. 2. **Growth arithmetic.** Because the current-account deficit is projected to widen and public consumption is pegged, **93% of the fiscal stimulus** to reach 4.2% growth must come from private investment, which GEAR assumes will respond to lower interest rates via a "crowding-out" story with **no empirical evidence** for South Africa. 3. **Interest-rate elasticity.** To deliver the projected jump in private investment from a deficit cut of only 0.3 pp of GDP, the implied interest-elasticity of investment is ~0.55, implausibly high, especially given mining's share. 4. **Fiscal consolidation.** The 3% deficit target is exogenous, not model-derived; SA's debt/GDP is moderate (56% vs 72% average OECD, 95% Japan) and external debt is low, no debt-trap case. 5. **Tax stance.** Ratio and econometric studies suggest SA was under-taxed by ~3% of GDP in 1994; the regressive VAT-heavy structure and absence of capital gains tax mean progressive reform could raise revenue and equity simultaneously. 6. **Monetary policy.** Hard-wiring inflation targeting into the growth framework is contractionary: SA never had an inflation surge, but the policy forces the Reserve Bank to tighten as soon as growth picks up. 7. **Exchange controls.** No rationale is provided for removing controls on domestic residents; the post-Finrand experience showed volatile short-term flows, not the promised FDI surge. 8. **Trade and industry.** Accelerated tariff cuts to "compensate" for the depreciation squander the opportunity to use the depreciation for import substitution; there is no real industrial policy, only "industrial support measures". 9. **Export targets.** An 8.4% average non-gold export growth rate implying a jump in the export/GDP ratio from 20.9% to 25.6% in four years has **no historical precedent** among major developed or developing economies. 10. **Labour market.** GEAR assumes inflexibility; the ILO country review (Standing, Sender, Weeks 1996) and NALEDI/LRS evidence show SA's labour market is already highly flexible in employment and wages. "Regulated flexibility" risks entrenching segmentation and widening the wage gap. 11. **Income distribution.** On GEAR's own numbers, real-wage growth (≈1%) lags GDP growth (4.2%) → wage share falls → inequality will **worsen**, contradicting the RDP's stated growth-path principle. 12. **No link from equity to growth.** Adelzadeh cites the 1996 UN Human Development Report: East Asian economies combined rapid growth with *low* inequality, undermining GEAR's implicit trickle-down premise. ## What the paper recommends - Use the **dynamic government budget constraint** to assess sustainability, not an exogenous 3% rule. - Use public productive expenditure to **crowd in** private investment. - Integrate monetary policy with industrial strategy (differential interest rates). - Reject any two-tier labour market; adopt a basic floor of rights. - Make any social accord **conditional on a growth and development framework**, not on wage restraint a

chunks.title: Sanctions Impact South African Exports
chunks.text: ## Bibliographic details - **Author:** Simon J. Evenett (World Trade Institute, University of Berne; CEPR) - **Published:** *Scottish Journal of Political Economy* 49(5), November 2002, pp. 557––573 ## Research question What was the effect of the sanctions imposed by eight industrialised economies (Denmark, France, Great Britain, Italy, Japan, Norway, Sweden, and the US) on South African exports between 1985/86 and 1991/93, and have SA exports recovered after the sanctions were lifted? ## Method - **Gravity equation** on bilateral trade flows, 1978–1999 (using World Bank / UN COMTRADE data). - Benchmark sample: SA plus 30 other upper-/lower-middle-income, non-communist developing economies with GDP > $1bn and population > 1m. - Sanction effect identified via **country-pair dummies** interacted with sanction periods, alongside distance, GDP, population. - Heavy emphasis on **outlier diagnostics** (DFBETAs): robustness checks exclude trade-partner pairs with unusually large or small bilateral trade relative to GDP. ## Main findings 1. Naïve estimates suggest **EC sanctions** had the largest adverse effect on SA exports. 2. Once outliers are removed, the **US Comprehensive Anti-Apartheid Act (CAAA) of 1986** is the single largest shock, reducing bilateral imports from SA by about **one third**. 3. US sanctions' effect **persists beyond 1991** (when CAAA was lifted); SA exports to the US do not bounce back, consistent with hysteresis from sunk costs of export relationships. 4. EC sanctions show some **"bounce-back"** after removal. 5. Estimated **distance elasticity falls by 50%** when outliers are dropped, a methodological point: gravity-equation distance estimates are often inflated by outliers. ## Policy / methodological implications - Sanctions can have **long-lived effects** on trade patterns, even after repeal, weakening the "remove sanctions → trade recovers" incentive argument. - Gravity-equation estimates in North–South samples are **sensitive to sample construction**; one must test robustness to outliers. - Consistent with [[sanctions-synthetic-control-south-africa|Uhuru (2020)]]'s finding that the macro effect of sanctions on GDP/cap persists well past 1994. ## Significance Provides the bilateral, sector-agnostic trade-flow evidence that complements the macro-level SCM study: trade partners that imposed CAAA-style sanctions persistently imported less from SA, and this persistence is a plausible micro-foundation for the lag effect observed in GDP per capita. ## Sources - `raw/readings/sanctions_impact_south_african_exports.pdf`, Evenett, S. J. (2002). "The Impact of Economic Sanctions on South African Exports," *Scottish Journal of Political Economy* 49(5): 557–573.

chunks.title: Sanctions Synthetic Control South Africa
chunks.text: ## Bibliographic details - **Author:** Malebo Uhuru (MLBUHU001), supervisor Dr Grieve Chelwa - **Institution:** Development Finance Centre (DEFIC), Graduate School of Business, University of Cape Town - **Year:** February 2020 (MCom dissertation, 70 pages) ## Research question What are the economic costs that South Africa suffered because of the UN/US/EC sanctions of 1985–1994, measured as the gap between actual and counterfactual GDP per capita? ## Method - **Synthetic Control Method (SCM)** à la Abadie & Gardeazabal (2003) and Abadie, Diamond & Hainmueller (2010, 2015). - Treated unit: South Africa (treatment period 1985–1994). - Counterfactual ("synthetic SA") built as a weighted average of donor countries that did not face comparable sanctions, matched on pre-treatment predictors (GDP per capita, investment, schooling, population, etc.). - Follows Gharehgozli (2017) on Iran. ## Main results - **Sanctions reduced SA's GDP per capita by about 30% relative to synthetic SA by 1998**, i.e. four years after sanctions were lifted. - The treatment effect is **largest after sanctions end**, indicating a substantial **lag effect** (hysteresis). - Placebo tests: SA's effect is larger than all donor-pool placebos except the Philippines; significant at the 10% threshold. - Results are robust to donor-pool composition (drop Mexico, Peru, Argentina separately). - A "false treatment" placebo dated to 1980 does not generate a significant gap, supporting the 1985 identification. ## Why the lag? The dissertation argues the lag reflects: - Persistent hysteresis in trade and investment relationships (foreign firms slow to return after divestment). - Reputational / risk-premium effects on capital inflows. - Delayed pass-through of supply-side shocks (industries that lost scale during the boycott take time to rebuild). - Consistent with Evenett's (2002) finding in [[sanctions-impact-south-african-exports|the gravity-equation paper]] that US-CAAA effects persist well after the 1991 repeal. ## Policy implications - For target nations: sanctions can have **long-lasting** economic consequences that outlive the sanctions regime itself. - For senders: sanctions are not costless policy tools; they can slow reconstruction for a decade. - For methodology: SCM is well-suited to single-unit policy shocks with long pre- and post-periods. ## Caveats - Does not assess the **humanitarian** cost or the **political** contribution of sanctions to ending apartheid (explicitly out of scope, cf. Levy 1999; Crawford & Klotz 1999). - Treats sanctions as a single binary treatment; does not separate trade vs financial vs arms sanctions. - Donor pool limited to middle-income non-communist economies. ## How it connects Pair with [[sanctions-impact-south-african-exports|Evenett (2002)]] for the bilateral-trade-flow channel and with the [[political-economy-of-transition|ILRIG handbook]] for the political-economy narrative of the 1985–1994 crisis. ## Sources - `raw/readings/sanctions_synthetic_control_south_africa.pdf`, Uhuru, M. (2020). *Evaluating the Impact of Economic Sanctions on South Africa: A Synthetic Control Approach*. MCom dissertation, UCT.

chunks.title: State Of Trade Policy South Africa
chunks.text: ## Bibliographic details - **Authors:** Rashad Cassim, Donald Onyango, Dirk Ernst van Seventer (Trade and Industrial Policy Strategies, TIPS) - **Published:** January 2004 (TIPS Monograph, ISBN 1-919982-04-3, ~177 pages) - **Peer reviewers noted:** Prof. Merle Holden (Univ. of Natal), Dr Carolyn Jenkins (Oxford) ## Purpose A comprehensive stock-taking of SA trade policy a decade after democracy: tariff structure, WTO commitments, effective protection, exchange-rate behaviour, welfare analysis, employment effects, and export competitiveness. It is the most detailed public-domain diagnostic of the SA tariff schedule c. 2000–2001. ## Periodisation of trade liberalisation - **First episode (1972–1977)**, modest. - **Second episode (1983–1990)**, replacement of quantitative restrictions with tariffs; Industrial Development Corporation (IDC) reforms. - **Post-1990 liberalisation**, phasing out of import surcharges; simplification of the tariff schedule; rationalisation of tariff lines. - **Post-1994 / WTO (1995)**, accelerated five-year tariff phase-down; GEAR (1996) committed to faster reductions beyond WTO obligations; SADC, EU-SA TDCA, and later SACU agreements. ## Key facts on tariffs - Tariff lines nearly halved relative to the 1980s. - By 2000–2001 the schedule is split between MFN, EU (TDCA), and SADC preference regimes; average **import-weighted tariffs** fell from double digits in 1996 to single digits by 2000. - Still a long tail of HS-8 lines with ad-valorem-equivalent duties above 40% (notably textiles, clothing, footwear, some auto components). - The chapter computes **ad-valorem equivalents** for specific and mixed tariffs, which substantially changes the distribution of effective protection. - Computes **nominal and effective rates of protection (ERP)** and an **anti-export bias** index by SIC v5 sector, showing higher ERPs in light manufacturing (clothing, auto, food processing) and an anti-export bias that compresses over the 1990s. ## Exchange rate - Chapter 4 documents nominal and real effective exchange rate indices (1995 = 100) and argues: - Real depreciation in 1996 and 2001 substantially augmented trade protection beyond scheduled tariffs. - Export performance responds positively to real depreciation but with lags and volatility penalties. - Real-exchange-rate instability depresses the wage-productivity pass-through and investment decisions. ## Trade and employment - Trade liberalisation is **not** identified as the dominant driver of manufacturing employment decline; decomposition suggests **technological change / skill-biased demand** explains a large share of the fall. - A partial welfare analysis (small-country assumption, sector-specific import-demand elasticities) finds net welfare gains from the 2000-level tariff schedule cuts, but modest relative to the employment losses in specific industries (notably clothing and textiles). ## Export competitiveness - Decomposes manufacturing exports by factor usage (capital-, labour-, natural-resource-intensive) and by market ("rising stars", "falling stars", "leading retreat", "lost opportunities") à la CEPII. - SA's **rising stars** are concentrated in natural-resource and capital-intensive manufactures (autos, metals, chemicals); **falling stars** cluster in labour-intensive categories (textiles, apparel). - Regional orientation shifts: rising share of intra-African (SADC) exports, falling share to traditional EU destinations in labour-intensive goods. ## Policy recommendations (chapter 7) - Continue to simplify and lower the tariff schedule, but target remaining anti-export bias sectors. - Complement tariff reduction with **export support** and industrial policy to offset employment losses in labour-intensive sectors. - Strengthen analytical capacity (IDC, DTI, BTT) for trade-policy formulation. - Anchor exchange-rate management for export-sector stability. ## Significance for the project This is the most detailed **tariff-and-protection diagnostic** in the know

chunks.title: Stats Sa Qlfs P0211 2023Q3
chunks.text: ## Bibliographic details - **Publisher:** Statistics South Africa (Stats SA), ISIbalo House, Pretoria - **Release:** 14 November 2023 - **Series:** P0211, Quarterly Labour Force Survey, Quarter 3 2023 (Jul–Sep) - **Format:** Statistical release + tables + sampling-variability tables (~133 pages) ## Why it matters The QLFS is the **authoritative** household-based labour-force measurement in South Africa, covering persons 15–64. It provides the **strict (official)** and **expanded** unemployment definitions and is the source for employment-by-industry, occupation, province, and education counts. It is what Stats SA feeds into World Bank WDI, ILO, and SARB. ## Key labour-market indicators, Q3 2023 | Indicator | Jul–Sep 2022 | Apr–Jun 2023 | Jul–Sep 2023 | |---|---|---|---| | Population 15–64 (000s) | 40 322 | 40 746 | 40 886 | | Labour force (000s) | 23 491 | 24 268 | 24 594 | | Employed (000s) | 15 765 | 16 346 | **16 745** | |, Formal (non-agric) | 10 835 | 11 329 | 11 616 | |, Informal (non-agric) | 2 971 | 3 029 | 3 058 | |, Agriculture | 873 | 894 | 956 | |, Private households | 1 088 | 1 093 | 1 116 | | Unemployed (000s) | 7 725 | 7 921 | **7 849** | | Not economically active | 16 831 | 16 478 | 16 292 | |, Discouraged work-seekers | 3 514 | 3 182 | 3 156 | | **Unemployment rate (%)** | 32.9 | 32.6 | **31.9** | | Absorption ratio (%) | 39.1 | 40.1 | 41.0 | | Labour-force participation (%) | 58.3 | 59.6 | 60.2 | ## Highlights from the release - Employment **increased by 399 000** q/q and by 979 000 y/y, the eighth consecutive q/q increase since Q4 2021. - Unemployment rate **fell by 0.7 pp to 31.9%**, primarily driven by movements from "not economically active" and "unemployed" into "employed". - Formal-sector gains (+287 000 q/q) concentrated in **Finance (+230 000), Construction (+122 000), and Community & social services (+101 000)**. - Formal-sector losses in **Manufacturing (−70 000)**, Transport, Mining, Trade, Utilities. - Informal-sector gains in Trade, Transport, and Manufacturing. - Year-on-year, Manufacturing lost **123 000** jobs; Utilities lost 3 000; all other major industries grew. ## Methodology notes - Sample: rotating panel of dwelling units, 15 000 across the country; response rate ~80%. - **Strict (official) unemployment:** persons 15–64 who (i) did not work in the reference week, (ii) actively searched, (iii) available to start. - **Expanded unemployment:** includes discouraged work-seekers (no longer actively searching but available). The Q3 2023 expanded rate is substantially higher than the 31.9% strict rate. - Estimates are calibrated to post-Census 2011 mid-year population estimates; revisions apply when population weights are updated. ## Use in the project - Source for the **national unemployment time series** in `raw/data/sa_wdi_panel.csv` and in the project website's charts. - Benchmark for **provincial unemployment** in the interactive map (the tooltip values are approximations of the Q4 2023 strict rates; the P0211 Appendix 1 Table 2.3 and 2.7 contain the authoritative provincial splits). ## Sources - `raw/readings/stats_sa_qlfs_p0211_2023q3.pdf`, Statistics South Africa (2023). *Quarterly Labour Force Survey, Quarter 3: 2023.* Statistical Release P0211, Pretoria.

chunks.title: Stats Sa Qlfs P0211 2025Q1
chunks.text: ## Bibliographic details - **Publisher:** Statistics South Africa, Pretoria - **Release:** 13 May 2025 - **Series:** P0211, Quarterly Labour Force Survey, Quarter 1 2025 (Jan–Mar) - **Format:** Statistical release + tables + sampling-variability tables (~132 pages) ## Key labour-market indicators, Q1 2025 | Indicator | Jan–Mar 2024 | Oct–Dec 2024 | Jan–Mar 2025 | |---|---|---|---| | Population 15–64 (000s) | 41 158 | 41 561 | 41 691 | | Labour force (000s) | 24 971 | 25 069 | 25 015 | | Employed (000s) | 16 745 | 17 078 | **16 787** | |, Formal (non-agric) | 11 544 | 11 679 | 11 434 | |, Informal (non-agric) | 3 082 | 3 328 | 3 345 | |, Agriculture | 941 | 924 | 930 | |, Private households | 1 178 | 1 146 | 1 078 | | Unemployed (000s) | 8 226 | 7 991 | **8 228** | | Not economically active | 16 188 | 16 492 | 16 676 | |, Discouraged work-seekers | 3 048 | 3 466 | 3 473 | | **Unemployment rate (%)** | 32.9 | 31.9 | **32.9** | | Absorption ratio (%) | 40.7 | 41.1 | 40.3 | | Labour-force participation (%) | 60.7 | 60.3 | 60.0 | ## Highlights from the release - Employment **fell by 291 000 q/q** to 16.8 million, the first Q1 decline since 2021. - Unemployment **rose by 237 000** to 8.2 million; unemployment rate **up 1.0 pp to 32.9%**. - Formal-sector employment **fell by 245 000**, losses concentrated in Trade (−133 000), Community & social services (−98 000), Construction (−79 000), Mining (−41 000), Manufacturing (−40 000). Gains in Transport (+73 000), Finance (+43 000), Utilities (+30 000). - Informal-sector employment **rose by 17 000**, gains in Community & social services (+54 000), Manufacturing (+41 000), Finance (+17 000). - Youth (15–34) NEET rate remains elevated (see Figure 9 in the release). ## Comparison to Q3 2023 Two observations from comparing the two QLFS releases in this knowledge base: 1. The **strict unemployment rate** is volatile around 32% (31.9% in Q3 2023 → 32.9% in Q1 2025), with no clear downward trend. 2. **Manufacturing employment** ended Q1 2025 at ~1.68m, roughly **100–200k above** the Q3 2023 trough but still well below 2005 levels (~1.4m was the 2005 peak using a comparable definition, see [[mayekiso-trade-liberalisation-privatisation|Mayekiso & Lamla (2025)]]), consistent with the [[premature-deindustrialisation|premature-deindustrialisation]] thesis. ## Use in the project - Latest (Q1 2025) reference point for the **national labour-force aggregates** used on the project website. - Source for updated industry/occupation splits in `raw/data/sa_wdi_panel.csv` annual extrapolations for 2024–2025. ## Sources - `raw/readings/stats_sa_qlfs_p0211_2025q1.pdf`, Statistics South Africa (2025). *Quarterly Labour Force Survey, Quarter 1: 2025.* Statistical Release P0211, Pretoria.

chunks.title: Trade Liberalization Local Labor Markets South Africa
chunks.text: ## Bibliographic details - **Authors:** Bilge Erten (Northeastern), Jessica Leight (American University), Fiona Tregenna (University of Johannesburg, DST/NRF Chair in Industrial Development) - **Working paper:** 25 April 2018 (later published in *Economic Development and Cultural Change*) - **Funding:** NRF South Africa; Tregenna's Chair ## Research question What was the **causal** impact of post-1994 tariff reductions on local labour market outcomes in South Africa, on employment, wages, formality, labour-force participation, migration, and take-up of government transfers? ## Method (Bartik / local-labour-markets) - Build a **district-level tariff-exposure shock**: weighted average of industry tariffs, weighted by **baseline (1994) industry employment shares**. - Exploits two sources of variation: (a) cross-industry variation in tariff cuts (textiles/clothing cut heavily; coal mining cut little), and (b) cross-district variation in industrial composition of employment. - Individual-level labour-force survey data, 1994–2004. - Same empirical strategy as Topalova (2007, 2010) for India; McCaig (2011) for Vietnam; Kovak (2013) for Brazil; Autor, Dorn & Hanson (2013) for the US "China shock". ## Headline results 1. Districts with larger tariff cuts experienced **significant declines in both formal and informal employment** relative to less exposed districts, driven primarily by **manufacturing** job losses. 2. **No wage effects** for those who remain employed, wages are too rigid (unions, sectoral bargaining councils) to absorb the shock. 3. Displaced workers **do not transition** into other tradable sectors or services, and **do not migrate** to less-affected districts. 4. Instead, they become **discouraged workers** or exit the labour force, raising **broad unemployment** and **non-participation**. 5. They become **more likely to receive government transfers** (disability grants, child-support grants, dependent-care grants), echoing the Autor-Dorn-Hanson China-shock finding for the US. 6. Heterogeneity: **less-educated black and coloured workers** face a higher probability of job loss among low-education groups; no systematic gender or age heterogeneity. 7. Robust to dropping districts with very high textile or coal employment shares, and to pre-trend checks. ## Why the SA adjustment looks different from India/Brazil The SA labour market has four unusual features: - Persistently very high baseline unemployment (28–35%). - Small informal sector with high barriers to entry (legacy of apartheid restrictions on black entrepreneurship, high compliance costs). - Wage rigidity via sectoral bargaining councils. - Under-developed manufacturing base even at the start of liberalisation. These features mean tariff-induced shocks cannot be absorbed through wage cuts, informalisation, or migration, so they translate into non-participation and safety-net dependence, a feature of the SA labour market that differs sharply from other middle-income liberalisers. ## Significance First **causal** evidence of the local labour-market effects of SA's 1994–2004 tariff liberalisation. It complements: - [[trade-liberalization-sa-manufacturing|Driver (2019)]], mark-up / productivity / exchange-rate channel. - [[state-of-trade-policy-south-africa|Cassim et al. (2004)]], tariff structure itself. - [[mayekiso-trade-liberalisation-privatisation|Mayekiso & Lamla (2025)]], 1994–2024 deindustrialisation narrative. - [[wealth-inequality-in-south-africa|Chatterjee, Czajka & Gethin (2021)]], the stock side of rising inequality. It is the single strongest causal piece of evidence in the knowledge base for the claim that **tariff liberalisation contributed to job losses** in SA's tradable sector, with the losses absorbed by non-participation and transfer-grant dependence rather than by informal or service-sector absorption. ## Sources - `raw/readings/trade_liberalization_local_labor_markets_south_africa.pdf`, Erten, B., Leight, J. and Tregenna, F. (2018). *Trade Libe

chunks.title: Trade Liberalization Sa Manufacturing
chunks.text: ## Bibliographic details - **Author:** Ciaran F. Driver, SOAS University of London - **Published:** UNU-WIDER Working Paper 2019/30 (April 2019), ISBN 978-92-9256-664-7 - **Project:** SA-TIED (Southern Africa, Towards Inclusive Economic Development) - **Open access, CC BY, hdl.handle.net/10419/211260** ## Research question Revisit the debate in *Economics of Transition* 2008 (Aghion & Fedderke eds.) on whether trade liberalisation boosted or harmed SA manufacturing, using updated Bureau of Economic Research (BER) survey data through to ~2017. ## The three camps it tests - **Aghion, Braun & Fedderke (2008):** SA manufacturing had high mark-ups that retarded productivity growth; more competition (via trade or other channels) raises productivity. - **Edwards & Lawrence (2008):** Trade liberalisation boosted "non-commodity manufacturing" exports by lowering input costs and reducing the relative profitability of domestic sales. - **Rodrik (2008):** Competitive pressure cut mark-ups, profitability, and manufacturing employment, a bad outcome given the "binding political constraint" of unemployment for black South Africans. ## Hypotheses re-examined - **A1:** No trend reduction in manufacturing mark-ups post-transition. - **A2:** More competition → higher productivity (up to an inflection point). - **EL1:** Trade liberalisation reduced import costs for manufacturing. - **EL2:** Trade liberalisation reduced the relative profitability of domestic sales and raised non-commodity manufacturing exports. - **R1:** The manufacturing mark-up falls in the post-transition period. - **R2:** Manufacturing employment falls as the mark-up falls. ## Main findings 1. A rising **real exchange rate** (real appreciation) lowers unit raw material costs (EL1-style), as expected, but also **compresses the mark-up**, exporter profitability does not improve because firms keep foreign-currency prices from rising. 2. Real appreciation **lowers export volumes**, supporting Rodrik's channel from exchange rate to manufacturing pricing power. 3. The evidence is **more consistent with Rodrik** than with Aghion et al.: competition and real-exchange-rate pressure do not translate into robust productivity or employment gains; they compress profitability and shrink the sector. 4. Manufacturing employment falls across the period, alongside a rise in the import-penetration ratio from 17% (1990) to ~42% (2012), higher in labour-intensive sectors. 5. Capital investment responds to demand and profitability, not to tariff policy directly, consistent with a "demand-constrained" view of manufacturing investment. ## Policy implication Driver concludes that "option (iii)", protecting or subsidising labour-intensive manufacturing activity, possibly via tax-wedge adjustments and selective protection, is more defensible than "option (iv)", further liberalisation to squeeze mark-ups. The paper is sympathetic to a **new-industrial-policy** / [[premature-deindustrialisation|premature-deindustrialisation]] view. ## Data - BER Manufacturing Survey micro-panel (ISIC 3) for mark-ups, input costs, export volumes, capacity utilisation. - Real effective exchange rate, tariffs, import penetration ratios from public macro sources. - Period: early 1990s to ~2017. ## Significance Driver is the most direct econometric test, using SA-specific firm-level data, of whether liberalisation actually did the good things GEAR assumed. The answer is: not much; the compression of mark-ups and employment is real, but productivity and export gains are modest and concentrated. A natural complement to [[trade-liberalization-local-labor-markets-south-africa|Erten-Leight-Tregenna (2018)]] (labour side) and [[state-of-trade-policy-south-africa|Cassim et al. (2004)]] (tariff-structure side). ## Sources - `raw/readings/trade_liberalization_manufacturing_south_africa.pdf`, Driver, C. F. (2019). *Trade Liberalization and South African Manufacturing: Looking Back with Data*. UNU-WIDER WP 2019/30.

chunks.title: Wealth Inequality Lab South Africa
