chunks.text: ## Bibliographic details - **Authors:** Aroop Chatterjee (Southern Centre for Inequality Studies, Wits); Léo Czajka (UC Louvain); Amory Gethin (World Inequality Lab / Paris School of Economics) - **Working paper:** WIL 2021/16 (June 2021; revised version of WIL 2020/06) - **Commissioned under:** UNU-WIDER SA-TIED project ## Research question What is the distribution of **personal household wealth** in South Africa between 1993 and 2017, and how has it evolved since the end of apartheid? ## Method - Combines **three micro-to-macro data streams**: 1. **Personal Income Tax microdata (SARS, 2010–2017)**, IRP5 (employer-reported wages, pensions) and ITR12 (self-assessed mixed/rental/interest/dividend income) covering the top ~30% of the population. 2. **Household surveys**, seven income surveys (1993–2015) and 54 quarterly Labour Force Surveys from 2000. 3. **Macroeconomic balance sheets**, SARB household-sector assets and liabilities (housing, pensions, life insurance, bonds, equity, currency; mortgage and other debt). - **Income-capitalisation approach** à la Saez & Zucman (2016) and Garbinti et al. (2018): capitalise observed income flows onto macro totals; rescale non-income-yielding assets (housing, currency) separately. - Harmonised yearly microfile, 1993–2017, reweighted to match macro totals. ## Headline results - The **top 10%** of wealth holders own **85–86%** of household wealth. - The **top 1%** owns **~55%**. - The **top 0.01%** (≈3 500 adults) owns a larger share of wealth than the **bottom 90%** (≈32 million adults) combined. - The **average wealth of the bottom 50% is negative**, their liabilities exceed their assets. - **No decline in wealth inequality** since the end of apartheid; if anything, the top 1% and top 0.01% shares drifted upwards. - Wealth concentration is higher than in any other country for which comparable high-quality estimates exist (France, UK, US, Russia, China, India). ## Asset composition - **Top 10%** own 55%+ of business assets and housing, and **over 99%** of bonds and stock. - **Top 0.1%** wealth is dominated by **financial assets**. - **Bottom 90%** wealth is mostly **owner-occupied housing and pension wealth**. - Life-cycle wealth accumulation exists but is dwarfed by differences in **initial endowments**, a strong inheritance channel. ## Methodological contribution - Shows that **income capitalisation + macro rescaling** yields substantially more realistic top-end wealth distributions than survey self-reports alone (which systematically under-report financial assets). - Provides a blueprint for developing-country wealth-inequality estimation where wealth surveys are weak but PIT and balance-sheet data exist. - Compares three methodologies (direct survey, rescaling, capitalisation) and shows capitalisation is most reliable at the top. ## Policy / framing implications - Wealth inequality is **structural**, inherited from apartheid asset accumulation, and has **not been meaningfully addressed** by 1994–2017 policy, supporting the core claim of the [[political-economy-of-transition|political-economy handbook]] that the economic settlement left asset distribution largely untouched. - Income-focused distributional statistics (e.g. Gini of ~0.63–0.67) understate the actual concentration at the very top once **financial and business assets** are properly measured. - Complements [[mayekiso-trade-liberalisation-privatisation|Mayekiso & Lamla (2025)]], their inequality story is a flow story; this is the stock story. ## Data availability The series is part of the **World Inequality Database (WID.world)**; replication code and microdata access via the SA-TIED data lab. ## Sources - `raw/readings/wealth_inequality_lab_south_africa.pdf`, Chatterjee, A., Czajka, L. and Gethin, A. (2021). *Wealth Inequality in South Africa, 1993–2017.* World Inequality Lab WP 2021/16.

chunks.title: Regression battery overview
chunks.text: The project ran 630 regression specifications on a national panel from 1990 to 2024. At the basic 5% cutoff, 364 were significant; 183 survived Bonferroni; 316 survived Benjamini–Hochberg (BH). HAC standard errors use 3 lags. These are associations, not proof of causation.

chunks.title: Trade and employment / inequality
chunks.text: Two conclusions survive strict BH corrections. First, trade openness lines up with decline in trade-exposed sectors: manufacturing employment share and tradable employment share both fall as trade openness rises (HAC t roughly -2.5 and -3.8 over 26 years). Second, trade openness tracks the rise of the top 1% income share. The Chow test does not find a clean structural break at the 1996 GEAR pivot. Trade does not robustly explain unemployment levels on its own.

chunks.title: About this project
chunks.text: The Price of Integration is an Economics 30 capstone by Sunmit Hallur (Spring 2026). It examines South Africa after 1994: whether reconnecting to global trade and finance spread jobs and income to most people. The central tension is integration without inclusion, trade and GDP per capita rose while unemployment stayed high, tradable sectors shrank, and income concentrated at the top. The site combines narrative sections, national charts, a provincial unemployment map, regression results with multiple-testing corrections, and the Two Lives interactive story.

chunks.title: Methods and data
chunks.text: The project uses World Bank WDI national panels (trade openness, GDP per capita, unemployment), labour force surveys (OHS, LFS, QLFS) for sector employment shares, wealth and top-income series from WID and related sources, and a provincial unemployment map built from consistent survey codes. A battery of time-series regressions uses HAC standard errors; Benjamini–Hochberg (BH) and Bonferroni correct for multiple testing. Results are described as associations, not proof of causation. District-level trade exposure work (Erten, Leight, Tregenna) is cited for sharper labour-market mechanisms. The Chow test does not find a clean break exactly at the 1996 GEAR pivot.

chunks.title: How the site is organized
chunks.text: Sections walk from the research question through ground-level context (From the Ground, Timeline), national economy (Macro, Sectors, Inequality), the Two Lives choices narrative, regression Evidence, the provincial Map, Conclusions, and Sources with papers and knowledge-base notes. Macro charts show trade openness vs GDP per capita and unemployment. Sectors highlight manufacturing and tradable employment loss. Inequality covers top shares and wealth concentration. Results summarize which specifications survive BH correction. The map compares provinces on unemployment with a fixed colour scale across years.

chunks.title: South Africa background (essay frame)
chunks.text: After apartheid ended in 1994, sanctions lifted and South Africa rejoined global trade and capital markets. The RDP (1994) emphasised housing, services, and redistribution; by 1996 policy shifted toward GEAR (fiscal tightening, lower tariffs, inflation targets, capital-account openness). This essay situates those policy turns alongside stylised facts: rising trade openness, modest GDP per capita growth, very high unemployment, declining manufacturing and tradable employment shares, and rising top-income concentration. It does not provide a full political history; see Sources for academic papers and World Bank updates.

chunks.title: Headline findings
chunks.text: Main takeaways: South Africa reconnected to the world faster than it shared the benefits. Trade openness and GDP per capita rose together; unemployment remained very high. Under strict BH correction, trade openness associates with lower manufacturing and tradable employment shares and with a higher top 1% income share; it does not robustly explain unemployment levels alone. Geography matters: eastern and former homeland provinces tend toward higher unemployment on the map. Two Lives illustrates how the same macro shocks can land differently for a white finance worker in Johannesburg vs a black factory worker in a Cape Town township.

chunks.title: Site timeline overview
chunks.text: The interactive timeline on this site walks key moments in post-apartheid South Africa: sanctions unwinding, 1994 elections and RDP, 1995 WTO entry, 1996 GEAR, the 2000s commodity boom, the 2008-09 financial crisis, widening inequality through the 2010s, 2017 sovereign credit downgrades, the COVID-19 shock, and latest QLFS unemployment readings.

chunks.title: 1989-93: Apartheid sanctions unravel
chunks.text: In 1989-93, Apartheid sanctions unravel. Trade and banking sanctions eased step by step while multiparty talks (CODESA) moved forward.

chunks.title: 1994: Democratic elections and RDP
chunks.text: In 1994, Democratic elections and RDP. First democratic elections after apartheid. The RDP emphasised redistribution and basic services; import tariffs were still relatively high.

chunks.title: 1995: Joining the WTO
chunks.text: In 1995, Joining the WTO. South Africa joined the World Trade Organization. Membership committed the country to phase down import taxes through about 2005.

chunks.title: 1996: GEAR adopted
chunks.text: In 1996, GEAR adopted. The government adopted GEAR: tighter budgets, lower trade barriers, some privatisation, and inflation targets became the main macro recipe.

chunks.title: 2000s: Commodity boom years
chunks.text: In 2000s, Commodity boom years. Resource prices and foreign investment jumped during the 2000s commodity boom; factory jobs outside mining often struggled.

chunks.title: 2008-09: Global financial crisis
chunks.text: In 2008-09, Global financial crisis. The global financial crisis hit manufacturing sharply; unemployment stepped up in South Africa.

chunks.title: 2009-18: The gap widens
chunks.text: In 2009-18, The gap widens. A decade of slow, uneven growth: asset-holders kept pulling ahead while jobless and informal workers fell further behind.

chunks.title: 2017: Sovereign rating downgrades
chunks.text: In 2017, Sovereign rating downgrades. Credit-rating agencies moved South Africa below top investment grades in 2017; government borrowing became costlier.

chunks.title: 2020-22: COVID-19 shock
chunks.text: In 2020-22, COVID-19 shock. COVID-19 brought record single-year job losses in 2020; only partial recovery followed through 2021-22.

chunks.title: 2024-25: QLFS Q1 2025 unemployment
chunks.text: In 2024-25, QLFS Q1 2025 unemployment. QLFS Q1 2025: narrow unemployment 32.9%; broader unemployment including discouraged seekers 43.1%; youth unemployment 46.1%.

chunks.title: Walkthrough: The Price of Integration
chunks.text: This essay is about South Africa after apartheid: the country rejoined the world economy when sanctions lifted after 1994. Trade and growth rose, but jobs and income did not spread evenly. The pattern in the data is that openness squeezed trade-exposed sectors while unemployment stayed high and the richest households kept more income.

chunks.title: Walkthrough: The research question
chunks.text: The question driving everything: Did opening up the country open it up for everyone? Short answer: not enough. After 1994 the RDP promised redistribution; by 1996 policy shifted to GEAR, with tighter budgets and lower trade barriers. The site focuses on what the numbers show over time.

chunks.title: Walkthrough: Places before averages
chunks.text: Before the national charts, the essay grounds you in places: Johannesburg's CBD, a township like Gugulethu, and Durban's port. One national average hides how apartheid's geography still shapes who gets good jobs and who does not.

chunks.title: Walkthrough: Timeline
chunks.text: The timeline walks key policy and economic moments from 1994 onward: democratisation, RDP, GEAR, trade liberalisation, and the long climb in trade openness. Use it as the narrative spine before you read the charts.

chunks.title: Walkthrough: National economy
chunks.text: Trade openness (exports plus imports divided by GDP) shoots up after the nineties. Income per person rises, but much more slowly. Unemployment stays high. When two lines move together that is correlation, not automatic proof that trade caused unemployment by itself.

chunks.title: Walkthrough: Sectors
chunks.text: This is the core of the project. Manufacturing lost about a third of its GDP share since 1990. Tradable sectors (farming, mining, factories) shed nearly twelve percentage points of employment share from 1999 to 2025. Services did not fully replace those losses.

chunks.title: Walkthrough: Inequality
chunks.text: Income and wealth stayed concentrated at the top. The top 10% income share rose sharply; wealth is even more skewed than income. Trade openness tracks the top 1% share in regressions that survive strict multiple-testing correction.

chunks.title: Walkthrough: Two Lives
chunks.text: Two Lives is an interactive story: Pieter in Johannesburg finance versus Sipho in a Cape Town township factory. You make choices through thirty years of integration. Same country, different starting points, different options.

chunks.title: Walkthrough: Evidence
chunks.text: Hundreds of regressions were run with HAC errors and multiple-testing corrections. What survives BH: trade openness with falling tradable employment and with rising top-income concentration. What does not: a clean GEAR break in 1996, or trade alone explaining unemployment.

chunks.title: Walkthrough: Map
chunks.text: One national unemployment rate hides huge provincial differences. The map uses the same colour scale every year so you can see real change. Poorer eastern provinces and former homelands tend to stay darker; Gauteng is lighter on average but still far from full employment.

chunks.title: Walkthrough: Conclusions
chunks.text: Four takeaways: opening showed up in headline numbers; it did not produce broad inclusion; the sector story is the sharper lens; policy should combine trade with jobs, training, and place-based investment. If you remember one line: South Africa reconnected to the world faster than it shared the benefits.

chunks.title: Two Lives interactive
chunks.text: Apartheid ends. Sanctions lift, the rand floats, capital controls ease, and South Africa rejoins the world economy. Two men start the new era on the same day, but not in the same place. Walk them both through the next thirty years. You make the calls; the country answers. Pieter: White · Johannesburg · a broker's salary and a paid-off house Under apartheid, the law reserved the good jobs, the suburbs, and the bank credit for people like Pieter. In 1994 he is 38, employed in finance, with savings and a network. Sipho: Black · Gugulethu, Cape Town · a textile-factory wage and a rented room Sipho was born into a township the law built to keep labour cheap and far away. In 1994 he is 38, working a machine on a clothing-factory floor, renting a room, with no savings to fall back on. Key beats: 1994: The vote, and the open door. The country reopens to global trade and investment. Foreign money flows back in. Local factories are still busy, for now. 1996: Cheaper imports arrive. The government tightens budgets and lowers import taxes. Cheaper clothing and goods pour in. Customers start buying from abroad instead of local factories. 2000s: The commodity boom. Resource prices and foreign investment surge. Mining and finance are minting money. Factory wages outside those sectors keep struggling. 2008–09: The global crisis hits. A worldwide financial crisis lands. Manufacturing shrinks sharply and unemployment steps up. Whoever you are, the ground moves. 2015–25: The long aftermath. Recovery is thin and uneven. Unemployment stays very high; the wealthiest keep pulling ahead. This last choice shapes where each man lands.

chunks.title: Pieter (Two Lives)
chunks.text: Pieter is a composite character in Two Lives: White · Johannesburg · a broker's salary and a paid-off house Under apartheid, the law reserved the good jobs, the suburbs, and the bank credit for people like Pieter. In 1994 he is 38, employed in finance, with savings and a network. He starts with apartheid-era advantages in jobs, suburbs, and bank credit.

chunks.title: Sipho (Two Lives)
chunks.text: Sipho is a composite character in Two Lives: Black · Gugulethu, Cape Town · a textile-factory wage and a rented room Sipho was born into a township the law built to keep labour cheap and far away. In 1994 he is 38, working a machine on a clothing-factory floor, renting a room, with no savings to fall back on. He faces trade shocks and factory decline from a township starting point.

chunks.title: How Two Lives works
chunks.text: Two Lives is an interactive narrative, not survey data. You make choices through beats from 1994 to 2020; each option nudges hidden scores for Pieter and Sipho. Endings fall into bands from falling behind to pulling ahead. Estimated share of South African adults for whom each option was realistically available, not a survey of choices people actually picked. No national dataset maps 1:1 onto these narrative beats; figures combine QLFS employment shares, FinScope savings/inclusion, stokvel participation, grant coverage, and migration proxies. Pieter lands secure far more often than Sipho because they start from different scores, not because the menu differs.

chunks.title: Provincial unemployment spread widened
chunks.text: Original finding from my provincial map: national narrow unemployment rose from 21.15% (1997 Q4) to 31.19% (2025 Q4), a change of about 10.04 percentage points. Over the same window the provincial spread (gap between the lowest- and highest-unemployment provinces) widened from 16.89 to 24.46 pp (+7.57 pp). Western Cape stayed the lowest-unemployment province and Eastern Cape the highest in both endpoints. This is descriptive spatial divergence from harmonised OHS/LFS/QLFS microdata, not official Stats SA tables. It complements district-level evidence cited in Sources; it is not proof of causation on its own.

chunks.title: Macro charts data
chunks.text: National time series cover trade openness (exports plus imports over GDP), GDP per capita indexed to 1990, and broad unemployment from WDI and labour surveys. After 1994 trade openness rose much faster than income per person while unemployment stayed high.

chunks.title: FAQ: what is this essay about
chunks.text: The Price of Integration is an Economics 30 capstone about South Africa after apartheid. It asks whether opening the economy after 1994 spread opportunity to most people. The data show trade and GDP per capita rose, but unemployment stayed high, tradable sectors (especially manufacturing) lost jobs and output share, and income concentrated at the top.

chunks.title: FAQ: what happened in 1994
chunks.text: In April 1994 South Africa held its first democratic elections after apartheid. Sanctions lifted, the rand could float, and the country rejoined global trade and finance. The new government launched the RDP (redistribution and services); policy shifted toward GEAR by 1996.

chunks.title: FAQ: what happened in 1996
chunks.text: In June 1996 the government adopted GEAR (Growth, Employment and Redistribution): tighter fiscal targets, lower import tariffs, inflation targeting, and more capital-account openness. It marked a shift away from the RDP's redistribution-first emphasis. The essay's regressions do not find a clean structural break exactly at 1996, but GEAR names the policy turn toward orthodox macro stabilisation.

chunks.title: FAQ: when did trade rise
chunks.text: Trade openness (exports plus imports as a share of GDP) climbed steadily after apartheid ended and sanctions lifted. On the site's macro charts it rises much faster than income per person from the mid-1990s onward (roughly 37% of GDP toward the mid-60s by the 2010s). That is correlation in national data, not proof trade alone caused every other trend.

chunks.title: FAQ: what is gear
chunks.text: GEAR (Growth, Employment and Redistribution) was South Africa's 1996 macro strategy: tighter fiscal policy, lower import tariffs, inflation targets, and capital-account liberalisation. It replaced the earlier RDP emphasis on redistribution and public investment. The essay does not treat GEAR as the sole villain; the Chow test does not find a clean break exactly at 1996.

chunks.title: FAQ: what is rdp
chunks.text: The Reconstruction and Development Programme (RDP, 1994) promised housing, services, and redistribution as part of growth. By 1996 policy shifted toward GEAR. The site maps both as context for the post-1994 opening, not as a full policy history.

chunks.title: FAQ: why unemployment
chunks.text: National unemployment stayed very high after 1994 even as trade openness and GDP per capita rose. Tradable sectors (agriculture, mining, manufacturing) shed employment share while services did not fully absorb displaced workers. Trade alone does not robustly explain unemployment in the regression battery; the sector story is the sharper lens.

chunks.title: FAQ: what does the map show
chunks.text: The map colours South Africa's nine provinces by unemployment using the same colour scale every year so you can compare over time. Poorer eastern provinces and former homeland regions tend to stay darker (higher unemployment); Gauteng is somewhat lighter on average but far from full employment.

chunks.title: FAQ: did unemployment diverge across provinces
chunks.text: Yes, on my harmonised map series. National unemployment rose about 10 percentage points from 1997 Q4 to 2025 Q4, while the gap between the lowest- and highest-unemployment provinces widened by roughly 8 points (Western Cape lowest, Eastern Cape highest throughout). That is descriptive spatial divergence that complements district-level studies; it is not proof that trade or policy caused the widening.

chunks.title: FAQ: remember one sentence
chunks.text: South Africa reconnected to the world faster than it shared the benefits. Integration raised trade and average income but left unemployment high and income concentrated at the top.

chunks.title: FAQ: what data do you use
chunks.text: National charts use World Bank WDI (trade, GDP per capita, unemployment). Sector shares draw on OHS, LFS, and QLFS. Inequality uses WID-style top shares. The map uses provincial unemployment from labour surveys with harmonised codes. Regressions are on a national panel with HAC errors and Benjamini–Hochberg multiple-testing correction.

chunks.title: FAQ: causation or correlation
chunks.text: The essay treats national regressions as associations, not proof that trade alone caused unemployment or inequality. Language prefers lines up with or correlates unless citing district-level trade exposure evidence. The Chow test does not show a clean structural break at 1996 GEAR.

chunks.title: FAQ: who are pieter and sipho
chunks.text: Two Lives follows Pieter (white, Johannesburg finance, apartheid-era advantages in jobs and credit) and Sipho (black, Gugulethu township, factory wage, rented room) from 1994 through policy and trade shocks. It is illustrative, not a representative sample, but shows how the same macro trends can diverge by place and race.

chunks.title: FAQ: what are the main findings
chunks.text: Integration without inclusion: trade openness and GDP per capita rose; unemployment stayed high; manufacturing and tradable jobs lost share; top incomes concentrated. BH-robust links include trade with tradable employment decline and top 1% share, not unemployment levels alone.

chunks.title: FAQ: sovereign rating downgrade 2017
chunks.text: In 2017 credit-rating agencies downgraded South Africa below top investment grades. Borrowing became costlier for government. The site places this on the timeline as part of the post-1994 integration story, not as the sole cause of unemployment or inequality.

chunks.title: FAQ: what happened in 2017
chunks.text: In 2017 sovereign credit-rating agencies moved South Africa below top investment grades, making government borrowing costlier. The timeline card on this site labels this Sovereign rating downgrades.

chunks.title: FAQ: covid unemployment shock
chunks.text: COVID-19 (2020-22) brought record single-year job losses in South Africa with only partial recovery through 2021-22. The unemployment chart marks a COVID shock around 2020 when narrow unemployment jumped sharply.

chunks.title: FAQ: what is two lives
chunks.text: Two Lives is an interactive story on this site: Pieter (Johannesburg finance) versus Sipho (Gugulethu factory worker). You choose paths from 1994 through trade and policy shocks. Characters are composites built from cited evidence, not real survey respondents.

chunks.title: FAQ: is two lives real data
chunks.text: No. Pieter and Sipho are composite illustrations built from the essay's evidence and history. Choice feasibility tags (~X% of South Africans could) combine QLFS, FinScope, and other proxies; they are not actual survey choices.

chunks.title: FAQ: what is the timeline
chunks.text: The timeline section walks key post-1994 moments: sanctions unwinding, 1994 elections and RDP, 1995 WTO, 1996 GEAR, commodity boom, 2008-09 crisis, widening gap, 2017 sovereign downgrades, COVID-19, and latest QLFS unemployment.

chunks.title: FAQ: wto south africa
chunks.text: South Africa joined the WTO in 1995, committing to phase down import taxes through about 2005. The site links this to the broader trade-liberalisation arc after apartheid.

chunks.title: FAQ: financial crisis 2008
chunks.text: The 2008-09 global financial crisis shrank South African manufacturing sharply and unemployment stepped up. The timeline and unemployment chart both reference this shock.
